data.title: NSI (ENXTAM:NSI) Losses Challenge Bullish Turnaround Narrative Heading Into Q1 2026
data.description:
NSI (ENXTAM:NSI) has opened 2026 with Q1 numbers that keep the focus firmly on its top line. Recent quarterly revenue figures have ranged between €11.6 million and €31.9 million, while basic EPS has shifted from a profit of about €1.93 to a loss of about €2.06 over the 2025 financial year. Over that stretch, revenue has moved between €11.6 million and €31.9 million per quarter and trailing twelve month basic EPS has changed from about €0.63 to a loss of about €0.51. This sets up the latest update as a check on whether margins are starting to stabilize or remain under pressure.

See our full analysis for NSI.

With the headline figures on the table, the next step is to weigh these results against the most common stories around NSI to see which narratives the numbers support and which they challenge.

See what the community is saying about NSI

Advertisement

Losses on trailing EPS and net income remain a key focus

  • On a trailing twelve month basis to Q4 2025, NSI reported basic EPS of about €0.51 loss and net income of €9.79 million loss on revenue of €87.7 million.
  • Analysts' consensus view highlights that NSI is currently unprofitable and has seen losses grow at about 33.6% per year over the past five years. This sits uncomfortably next to the view that redevelopment of prime Amsterdam and Utrecht assets should support more stable earnings over time.
    • Consensus commentary points to portfolio quality and sustainability efforts as supports for tenant retention. However, the trailing loss and prior quarters such as Q4 2025, with net income of €40.3 million loss, show that recent profitability has not reflected that story.
    • Analysts also point to active asset recycling and green financing access as supports for earnings stability, while the trailing loss and unstable dividend record underline that stability has not yet appeared in the reported figures.

Revenue base is steady while earnings forecasts point to a turnaround

  • Over the last six reported quarters, quarterly revenue has stayed in a band of about €11.5 million to €31.9 million, and trailing twelve month revenue has sat between about €85.9 million and €87.7 million.
  • Bullish investors argue that earnings growth forecasts of about 13.41% per year and an expected move into profitability within three years fit with NSI's push into higher quality, sustainable office assets. Yet the largely flat trailing revenue range and current unprofitable status show that the forecasted step up in earnings has not yet appeared in the historical numbers.
    • The consensus narrative points to projects like Vitrum and HNK Rotterdam Alexander as potential sources of stronger occupancy and rents, while recent quarters such as Q3 and Q4 2025 have swung between net income of €37.75 million and a loss of €40.3 million, highlighting how variable earnings have been.
    • Analysts also expect revenue to decline modestly by about 0.3% per year over the next three years even as margins are expected to increase, so bulls are relying on margin improvement rather than top line expansion, which is not yet visible in the historical revenue band.
On these numbers, it helps to compare the earnings path and the redevelopment story against what other investors are already assuming about NSI's future, so you can see where your expectations differ from the crowd. 🐂 NSI Bull Case

Mixed valuation signals and balance sheet pressure

  • NSI trades on a P/S of about 4x, below peers at 7.1x and the European Office REITs industry at 7.5x, while its share price of €17.74 sits above a DCF fair value of about €13.57 and its debt is not well covered by operating cash flow.
  • Bears argue that weak cash flow coverage of debt, an unstable dividend record and current unprofitability, alongside the share price trading above DCF fair value, leave limited room for error even if the lower P/S multiple hints at relative value versus peers.
    • The risk view notes that leverage coverage is a major concern, and when combined with expectations for modest revenue decline and recent trailing net income loss of €9.79 million, the lower P/S ratio can be read as the market pricing in these balance sheet pressures.
    • At the same time, the mixed picture of relative valuation versus DCF fair value, along with widening losses over five years, fits with the concern that higher finance costs and redevelopment spending could weigh on future earnings if leasing outcomes do not match current expectations.
Skeptical investors who focus on debt coverage and the gap to DCF fair value may want to see how a more cautious case frames these same figures before deciding how much risk fits their portfolio. 🐻 NSI Bear Case

data.title: OVS (BIT:OVS) Margin Compression Challenges Bullish Growth Narratives In Latest Half
data.description:
OVS (BIT:OVS) opened FY 2026 with first half revenue of €825.7 million and basic EPS of €0.07, setting the tone for how its earnings story is evolving. The company has seen revenue move from €794.8 million in 1H FY 2025 to €910.8 million in 2H FY 2025 and then to €825.7 million in 1H FY 2026. Basic EPS shifted from €0.08 to €0.12 and then to €0.07 across the same periods, which puts the spotlight firmly on how efficiently that top line is being turned into profit. With trailing 12 month margins under pressure, this latest print keeps investor attention squarely on profitability and how much room there is for further compression or improvement.

See our full analysis for OVS.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to set them against the most common market narratives around OVS to see which storylines the latest earnings support and which they push back on.

See what the community is saying about OVS

Advertisement

Margins Ease Back To 2.7%

  • On a trailing 12 month view, net profit margin sits at 2.7%, compared with 3.1% a year earlier, on €1.7b of revenue and €46.6 million of net income.
  • Consensus narrative points to new Piombo lines and the B. Angel initiative as growth drivers, yet the margin drift tests how strong that bullish angle really is.
    • Piombo and B. Angel are linked to higher sell through and solid cash margins, while trailing earnings on this €1.7b revenue base are €46.6 million, which leaves only a thin profit layer to absorb cost swings.
    • Rising SG&A and items like higher labor costs and tax rates are highlighted as pressure points, and the move from a 3.1% to 2.7% margin shows those costs are still showing up in the numbers.

Bulls are betting that brand extensions and B. Angel momentum will outweigh cost pressures. It therefore helps to see how other investors connect those stories to the latest figures through the community view on OVS: See what the community is saying about OVS

2.6% Revenue Growth Trails Market

  • Over the last 12 months, revenue growth of 2.6% per year sits below the 5.7% per year Italian market rate, even though trailing revenue has reached about €1.7b.
  • Critics highlight that while the consensus narrative talks about international expansion in markets like Mexico and Japan and a 20% lift in the beauty segment in 2024, the 2.6% revenue growth rate still lags the broader market.
    • Planned international deals are expected, in that narrative, to add 10% to 15% to international sales, yet the trailing growth figure shows only modest progress so far against a 5.7% market backdrop.
    • The same narrative links beauty and younger customer initiatives to higher growth, but current reported revenue growth staying at 2.6% suggests those efforts are not yet showing up as outperformance versus the wider Italian market.

P/E Of 27.3x And DCF Gap

  • The stock trades on a 27.3x P/E, above both the 21.9x peer average and 18.5x European Luxury average, while the current share price of €5.22 sits well above the DCF fair value of about €1.90.
  • Bears argue that modest growth and thinner margins do not sit comfortably with this higher P/E and the gap between €5.22 and the €1.90 DCF fair value estimate.
    • Trailing net profit margin at 2.7% and revenue growth at 2.6% per year leave little cushion if costs stay elevated, which lines up with worries about SG&A and tax pressures in the narrative.
    • The unstable dividend record, combined with a P/E premium to peers and the DCF fair value sitting below the current price, supports the cautious view that a lot is already priced in relative to recent financial trends.

data.title: Is It Time To Reconsider Morningstar (MORN) After Its Recent Share Price Rebound?
data.description:
• Wondering whether Morningstar at around US$184.28 is starting to look attractive again, or if recent weakness is still baking in too much optimism.
  • The share price has moved 9.7% over the past week and 0.6% over the last month, yet the year to date return of 12.4% and 1 year return of 32.9% are both negative. This may catch the eye of investors watching for a potential reset in expectations.
  • Recent coverage has focused on how the stock's current level compares with its past performance and what that might mean for sentiment toward information and analytics providers. This context matters because it shapes how investors interpret both the recent short term rebound and the longer term drawdown.
  • Morningstar currently holds a valuation score of 3 out of 6. The rest of this article will walk through what that means across different valuation approaches, before finishing with an even more practical way to think about the price you are paying.

Find out why Morningstar's -32.9% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Morningstar Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much profit a company is expected to generate above the return required by its shareholders, then translates that into a per share value today. Instead of focusing on cash flows, it asks whether the business earns enough on its equity base to justify its price.

For Morningstar, book value is $30.75 per share and the stable book value estimate is $38.41 per share, based on weighted future book value estimates from 2 analysts. Using a median return on equity from the past 5 years, the model arrives at a stable EPS of $6.83 per share. Against a cost of equity of $3.15 per share, this implies an excess return of $3.68 per share, with an average return on equity of 17.78%.

Putting these inputs together, the Excess Returns model suggests an intrinsic value of about $115.44 per share, compared with the recent share price of around $184.28. This implies the stock screens as 59.6% overvalued on this approach.

Result: OVERVALUED

Our Excess Returns analysis suggests Morningstar may be overvalued by 59.6%. Discover 59 high quality undervalued stocks or create your own screener to find better value opportunities.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Morningstar.

Approach 2: Morningstar Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like Morningstar, the P/E ratio is a straightforward way to link what you pay for each share to the earnings that support that price. It lets you see how many dollars of price you are paying for each dollar of current earnings.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on how quickly earnings are expected to grow and how risky those earnings look. Higher growth and lower perceived risk usually support a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher uncertainty tend to justify a lower one.

Morningstar currently trades on a P/E of 19.49x. That sits below both the Capital Markets industry average P/E of 41.61x and a peer group average of 24.63x. Simply Wall St also calculates a proprietary “Fair Ratio” of 13.53x for Morningstar, which reflects factors such as its earnings growth profile, industry, profit margins, market value and risk characteristics.

This Fair Ratio can be more informative than a simple comparison with peers or the broad industry, because it is tailored to Morningstar’s own fundamentals rather than relying on broad group averages. Comparing the current P/E of 19.49x with the Fair Ratio of 13.53x suggests the shares are pricing in a richer multiple than the model implies.

Result: OVERVALUED

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Morningstar Narrative

Earlier it was mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation, so this is where Narratives come in.

A Narrative is simply the story you believe about a company, translated into numbers like fair value, future revenue, earnings and margins so your view is clear and measurable. On Simply Wall St, Narratives sit in the Community page and link that story to a financial forecast, then to a fair value that you can compare directly with the current share price. Narratives are easy to update when fresh information appears, such as new earnings or news, so your fair value view can change as the facts change rather than staying fixed. For Morningstar, one investor might build a Narrative that supports a much higher fair value than the current price, while another might set a much lower fair value, which shows how different assumptions about growth, profitability and risk can lead to very different conclusions about the same stock.

data.description:
First Financial Bankshares (FFIN) has just posted its Q1 2026 update with trailing twelve month revenue at US$603.0 million and basic EPS at US$1.77, alongside earnings growth of 13.5% over the last year. Over recent periods, revenue has moved from US$536.9 million to US$602.9 million while basic EPS has gone from US$1.56 to US$1.77, with net profit margin at 42.1% versus 41.6% a year earlier. This means the story is very much about how those margins shape the quality of the latest results for shareholders.

42.1% net margin and cost ratios

  • On a trailing basis, First Financial Bankshares reported a 42.1% net profit margin with a cost to income ratio around 45.5% for 2025, compared with 47.2% in the 2024 trailing figures.
  • Bulls often highlight the bank’s focus on relationship banking and diversified services, and the margin data gives that view something concrete to point to, with:
    • Net interest margin in recent quarters sitting near 3.8% and a trailing net profit margin just above last year’s 41.6%, which fits a narrative of efficient core banking.
    • Cost to income drifting around the mid 40% range across the trailing periods, which suggests expenses have not run away from revenue in the recent data.

Loan book around US$8.2b and asset quality

  • Total loans at the end of 2025 stood at US$8.2b with non performing loans at US$56.0 million on both the quarterly and trailing twelve month views.
  • Supporters of the bullish story around long term franchise strength may point to this pairing of loan size and credit quality, and the figures show:

    • That combination of scale and contained problem loans is often used by bullish investors to argue that the bank’s credit risk profile is manageable based on the supplied numbers.

P/E premium against peers and DCF gap

data.title: GE Vernova Weighs High Risk Markets Against Long Term Growth Potential
data.description:
• GE Vernova (NYSE:GEV) is preparing to expand energy operations into high risk markets including Venezuela, Iran, and Ukraine.
  • The company plans to pursue power infrastructure projects in these geopolitically sensitive regions as part of a broader international push.
  • This move introduces additional operational, regulatory, and reputational considerations compared with its existing footprint.

For investors watching GE Vernova at a share price of $978.32, this new international push comes after a period of strong stock performance, with the company up 44.0% year to date and 203.0% over the past year. The shift into higher risk regions adds a fresh dimension to the story around NYSE:GEV. Until now, the narrative has been more centered on legal issues and nuclear related partnerships.

Looking ahead, the key question for you is how this planned exposure to volatile and in some cases sanctioned countries may reshape the company’s risk and opportunity mix. Execution, regulatory approvals, and the ability to manage geopolitical shocks will likely be central to how investors judge this new phase of GE Vernova’s growth ambitions.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for GE Vernova by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on GE Vernova.

2 things going right for GE Vernova that this headline doesn't cover.

This push into Venezuela, Iran, and Ukraine signals that GE Vernova is willing to take on higher political and regulatory risk to win power and grid projects that many peers may avoid. It also aligns with management’s focus on regions with pressing energy security needs, where mobile power units, gas turbines, and grid equipment can be critical. For you as an investor, the key trade off is between access to complex, potentially higher margin infrastructure work and the added exposure to sanctions regimes, contract enforceability issues, and physical security. With GE Vernova already juggling a US$150b backlog, legal disputes in offshore wind, and rising demand linked to AI data centers, expanding into these markets increases execution complexity compared with peers such as Siemens Energy, Hitachi Energy, or Mitsubishi Power that also compete for large grid and generation projects.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The GE Vernova Narrative

  • Entering conflict affected and sanctioned markets could support the narrative’s focus on growing the installed base and service revenue, as emergency and reconstruction projects often require long term support contracts.
  • At the same time, these projects add to the existing concerns around large, lumpy infrastructure exposure, reinforcing analyst warnings about project risk, tariffs, and restructuring execution.
  • The narrative focuses heavily on order momentum and margin improvement but gives less attention to the specific geopolitical and sanctions risk that comes with operating in places like Venezuela and Iran.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for GE Vernova to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Expanding into high risk jurisdictions increases exposure to sanctions changes, contract disputes, and payment collection issues on already complex long duration projects.
  • ⚠️ Analysts have already highlighted vulnerability to large project delays and tariffs, and adding geopolitically sensitive work could make earnings more uneven if projects are paused or re scoped.
  • 🎁 If projects proceed as planned, GE Vernova could deepen its installed base in regions that may require ongoing maintenance, grid upgrades, and replacement equipment over many years.
  • 🎁 Experience in difficult environments could differentiate GE Vernova versus competitors like Siemens Energy and Hitachi Energy when governments and multilaterals look for contractors with a proven track record in crisis and post conflict settings.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, keep an eye on how quickly early discussions in Venezuela, Iran, and Ukraine turn into disclosed contracts, and whether those deals come with clear terms around sanctions compliance and payment security. Watch management commentary on how these projects are ring fenced from a risk control perspective and whether they change the mix of GE Vernova’s backlog by region or project type. Any updates around political developments, export controls, or legal disputes in these markets could become important markers for how much of this high risk opportunity actually converts into consistent revenue and service work.

data.title: Investors Shouldn't Be Too Comfortable With Nakamoto PacksLtd's (TSE:7811) Earnings
data.description:
Nakamoto Packs Co.,Ltd. (TSE:7811) just reported some strong earnings, and the market reacted accordingly with a healthy uplift in the share price. We did some analysis and think that investors are missing some details hidden beneath the profit numbers.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

Advertisement

The Impact Of Unusual Items On Profit

Importantly, our data indicates that Nakamoto PacksLtd's profit received a boost of JP¥319m in unusual items, over the last year. While we like to see profit increases, we tend to be a little more cautious when unusual items have made a big contribution. When we analysed the vast majority of listed companies worldwide, we found that significant unusual items are often not repeated. And that's as you'd expect, given these boosts are described as 'unusual'. Assuming those unusual items don't show up again in the current year, we'd thus expect profit to be weaker next year (in the absence of business growth, that is).

That might leave you wondering what analysts are forecasting in terms of future profitability. Luckily, you can click here to see an interactive graph depicting future profitability, based on their estimates.

Approach 1: Match Group Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model estimates what a business could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to today using a required rate of return. It is essentially asking what the stream of future cash coming to shareholders might be worth in current dollars.

For Match Group, the model uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on cash flow projections in $. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $1.02b. What counts as a “normal” or “fair” P/E depends on how quickly earnings are expected to grow and how risky those earnings appear. Higher growth and lower perceived risk can justify a higher multiple, while slower growth or higher uncertainty usually imply a lower one.

data.title: We're Not Very Worried About EyeGene's (KOSDAQ:185490) Cash Burn Rate
data.description:
We can readily understand why investors are attracted to unprofitable companies. For example, although Amazon.com made losses for many years after listing, if you had bought and held the shares since 1999, you would have made a fortune. But while history lauds those rare successes, those that fail are often forgotten; who remembers Pets.com?

Given this risk, we thought we'd take a look at whether EyeGene (KOSDAQ:185490) shareholders should be worried about its cash burn. In this report, we will consider the company's annual negative free cash flow, henceforth referring to it as the 'cash burn'. The first step is to compare its cash burn with its cash reserves, to give us its 'cash runway'.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Advertisement

When Might EyeGene Run Out Of Money?

A cash runway is defined as the length of time it would take a company to run out of money if it kept spending at its current rate of cash burn. As at December 2025, EyeGene had cash of ₩45b and no debt. Importantly, its cash burn was ₩7.2b over the trailing twelve months. Therefore, from December 2025 it had 6.3 years of cash runway. Even though this is but one measure of the company's cash burn, the thought of such a long cash runway warms our bellies in a comforting way. Depicted below, you can see how its cash holdings have changed over time.

Check out our latest analysis for EyeGene

How Well Is EyeGene Growing?

EyeGene managed to reduce its cash burn by 58% over the last twelve months, which suggests it's on the right flight path. And it could also show revenue growth of 6.3% in the same period. It seems to be growing nicely. Of course, we've only taken a quick look at the stock's growth metrics, here. You can take a look at how EyeGene has developed its business over time by checking this visualization of its revenue and earnings history.

How Hard Would It Be For EyeGene To Raise More Cash For Growth?

We are certainly impressed with the progress EyeGene has made over the last year, but it is also worth considering how costly it would be if it wanted to raise more cash to fund faster growth. Companies can raise capital through either debt or equity. Many companies end up issuing new shares to fund future growth. We can compare a company's cash burn to its market capitalisation to get a sense for how many new shares a company would have to issue to fund one year's operations.

EyeGene's cash burn of ₩7.2b is about 9.7% of its ₩74b market capitalisation. Given that is a rather small percentage, it would probably be really easy for the company to fund another year's growth by issuing some new shares to investors, or even by taking out a loan.

Is EyeGene's Cash Burn A Worry?

It may already be apparent to you that we're relatively comfortable with the way EyeGene is burning through its cash. In particular, we think its cash runway stands out as evidence that the company is well on top of its spending. On this analysis its revenue growth was its weakest feature, but we are not concerned about it. After taking into account the various metrics mentioned in this report, we're pretty comfortable with how the company is spending its cash, as it seems on track to meet its needs over the medium term.
